raw_case:
Cough-Variant Asthma in a Myasthenia Gravis Patient on Treatment With Steroids and Rituximab
Muscle weakness that worsens on exercise and improves on resting is the hallmark of myasthenia gravis (MG), an uncommon autoimmune illness. Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, immunosuppressants, and corticosteroids are commonly used in treatment. Rituximab has been tried in refractory patients. On the contrary, asthma is characterized by a persistent inflammation of the airways and a range of symptoms, including cough-variant asthma (CVA), in which the main symptom is only cough.We describe the case of a female in her 30s who had a history of MG and had been treated with steroids and rituximab. The patient had an acute dry cough in bouts. Spirometry revealed no obstructive or restrictive pattern, and the chest X-ray was normal. Based on allergy diathesis, a diagnosis of CVA was hypothesized and confirmed clinically by the patient’s reaction to long-acting beta-agonists and inhaled corticosteroids.This case emphasizes how difficult it can be to diagnose respiratory symptoms in MG patients and how crucial it is to rule out CVA as a differential diagnosis, particularly in patients who have recently started immunomodulatory medication and have an allergy tendency. Fast symptom relief and positive clinical results were achieved by the efficient use of inhalation treatment.

Introduction

Myasthenia gravis (MG) is an autoimmune disease characterized by muscle weakness that worsens with exertion and improves on rest. The pathogenesis of MG is due to the reduction in the number and activity of acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction which leads to a decrease in end-plate potential, manifesting as a suboptimal release of acetylcholine during repetitive muscle activity and resulting in muscle weakness [1]. Treatment options for MG include acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, immunosuppressants (azathioprine as an initial choice), corticosteroids, thymectomy, plasmapheresis, and intravenous immunoglobulin [2]. Although most patients, around 80% to 85%, respond well to available treatment options, 15% show poor response to conventional treatment options and require alternative forms of treatment such as rituximab, eculizumab, and cyclophosphamide [3].In some adults and children, cough may be the only predominant symptom of asthma, and the evidence of airflow limitation may be absent apart from bronchial provocation testing; such a condition is known as cough-variant asthma (CVA). Some patients also develop wheezing and bronchodilator responsiveness in this variant [4].Both MG and CVA are distinct entities. The coexistence of CVA and MG is a relatively rare occurrence. Exact prevalence rates in terms of percentages can be challenging to determine due to the limited number of reported cases and studies focusing on this specific combination. Although the main effects of MG are on neuromuscular function and the main effects of CVA are on respiratory function, congruent presentation of both conditions is bound to bring uncommon complications and challenges in management. The primary challenge is that the respiratory muscles may be impacted by MG, making diagnosing and managing asthma symptoms more difficult.Some drugs used to treat asthma, such as beta-agonists, can worsen muscle weakness in MG patients. Oral corticosteroids and biologicals are all part of asthma treatment, which may also be used to treat MG. Thus, the overlap of medications leads to variable manifestations of disease suppression or aggravation, making management of such a case perplexing.

Case presentation

A female in her 30s presented to the Respiratory Medicine outpatient department (OPD) with a one-week history of cough and throat discomfort. The cough occurred in bouts and was dry in nature. The patient also complained of giddiness during bouts of coughing.The patient had complaints of muscle weakness for eight years and was diagnosed with MG. The patient was on steroids and biological medications for the same. She did not suffer from any other comorbidities. The patient was treated with intravenous rituximab for MG and received two cycles a month before presentation.On examination, her vitals were within normal limits, and she was able to maintain a peripheral oxygen saturation of 97%. Respiratory system examination revealed normal vesicular breath sounds on auscultation in bilateral lung fields with no adventitious sounds. Other systemic examinations did not reveal any obvious abnormalities. The patient was admitted to the ward from OPD for further evaluation.The initial presentation of the patient led us to suspect a pathology of infective etiology either due to a bacterial, tubercular, fungal, or viral agent as the patient had received steroids and biologicals. However, the chest X-ray did not reveal any pleuro-parenchymal abnormality (Figure 1), and complete blood counts, including the absolute eosinophil counts, turned out to be normal.The patient’s functional capability was also assessed with a six-minute walk test that she could not complete because of excessive coughing. Her spirometry revealed a normal study (forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV1)/forced vital capacity (FVC) measured was 84.5%, FEV1% predicted was 82 L, and FVC% predicted was 80 L) with no obstruction or restriction (Figure 2).A transthoracic echocardiography was performed, which showed that the ejection fraction was normal and there were no regional wall motion or valve abnormalities. This was followed up with advanced imaging modalities such as a high-resolution CT of the thorax, which was also suggestive of no pleuro-parenchymal abnormality (Figure 3). These investigations essentially ruled out our suspicions of a pathology that would have been of an infective origin.This led us to elicit a more comprehensive and detailed clinical history from the patient, during which she gave hints of a few allergic diatheses such as episodic sneezing and rhinorrhea since childhood. She also gave a history of having previously worked as a general help in a woodcarving factory. Our patient was also obese with a body mass index of 30.2 kg/m². Taking these risk factors and additional information into account, we confirmed CVA as our clinical diagnosis.Based on our diagnosis, she was started on inhaled corticosteroids and long-acting beta-agonists. The patient’s rapid and positive response to bronchodilators within 24 hours supported the diagnosis of CVA. This was proof that our diagnosis of CVA was correct. The patient was prescribed a combination of formoterol and budesonide inhaler at the time of discharge.The patient was followed up bi-weekly until the complete resolution of symptoms and is on regular follow-up. She has reported a better quality of life and overall confidence since being on regular treatment.

Discussion

An occult form of asthma in which chronic cough is the only visible symptom is known as CVA [5]. This variant of asthma is in itself a challenge to diagnose because of multiple possible differential diagnoses, such as other variants of asthma, eosinophilic bronchitis, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, gastroesophageal reflux disease, upper airway cough syndrome or rhinosinusitis, pulmonary fibrosis, and bronchiectasis, all possible pathologies in a patient presenting with chronic cough [6].MG, a chronic autoimmune disease, is characterized by quick exhaustion and weakening of the voluntary muscles, including the ones involved in respiration. The weakening of the diaphragm and intercostal muscles can eventually lead to respiratory failure [7,8]. Many patients succumbed to pneumonia and respiratory failure before the introduction of acetylcholinesterase [9-11]. Thus, MG patients presenting with respiratory complaints are almost always first suspected of having an infection of the parenchyma and started on empirical antibiotics. Other lung pathologies such as asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, or emphysema are often missed on first presentation. In most asthmatics, oral steroids are a part of regularly prescribed medications [12]. In patients already on steroids, an acute exacerbation of asthma would not be a major differential. According to the Global Initiative for Asthma guidelines, CVA is generally refractory to treatment with steroids, as seen in our case [13].MG and asthma share immunopathogenic mechanisms. Previous research has revealed that MG patients have increased serum levels of sCD23. These patients also have overexpression of CD23 in the thymus germinal centers [14]. Substantial improvement in clinical outcomes has been observed following thymectomy which results in decreased serum levels of sCD23. CD23 is known to regulate antigen presentation, IgE production, and B-cell activation. CD23 is sometimes referred to as the low-affinity receptor for IgE (FcεRII). It is thought that IgE plays a role in the symptoms of asthma and other allergies. Dysregulation of CD4 T lymphocytes (Th1, Th2, Th17, and Teg) is believed to be linked to the pathophysiology of MG and asthma. The most significant correlation has been found between HLA genes and the genetic predispositions for MG and asthma, which have been extensively studied. Virus infections play a significant role in initiating and exacerbating asthma attacks as well as acute wheezing episodes in infants. Viral infection and its association with MG have also been suspected. Antiviral mechanisms can be visualized in the thymus of an MG patient. Certain mechanisms have been proposed to explain how viral agents induce MG, including molecular mimicry, cryptic antigens, epitope dissemination, bystander activation, and polyclonal activation [15].Patients with asthma can have breathlessness as a result of both airway obstruction and respiratory muscle weakness due to MG. If respiratory muscle weakness is not assessed in such patients, they may receive excessive treatment solely for respiratory issues. This can result in underdiagnosis or delay in the diagnosis of critical neuromuscular conditions. Autoimmune conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, systemic sclerosis, and Sjögren syndrome have been associated with airway hyperresponsiveness, most likely due to structural damage and inflammatory infiltration. Epidemiological studies have also shown a connection between asthma and autoimmunity in type I diabetes [16].Despite challenges in de-escalating medication regimens, neuromuscular disorders such as MG are often overlooked in patients presenting with acute asthma exacerbations. Both conditions include autoantibodies and have been demonstrated to coexist with other autoimmune disorders such as chronic idiopathic urticaria, inflammatory bowel disease, and autoimmune thyroid disease. MG has been associated with a severe type of non-atopic asthma. Exacerbations of both MG and asthma can lead to fatal consequences. In addition to being a major cause of acute asthma attacks, viruses have also been demonstrated to initiate several autoimmune disorders by molecular mimicry, self-antigen exposure, and tissue destruction. Furthermore, in both conditions, the respiratory muscles are mechanically impaired. It has been demonstrated that MG by itself can manifest as restrictive pulmonary disease. Thus, significant dyspnea may result from the coexistence of MG and asthma. For asthma patients who have difficulty with de-escalation of steroid medication, we should take into account neuromuscular illnesses such as MG [17].

Conclusions

This case illustrates the diagnostic challenge of managing MG alongside CVA, as both conditions can impact respiratory function and are complicated by overlapping symptoms and treatments. The patient’s positive response to inhaled corticosteroids and beta-agonists confirmed the diagnosis of CVA, highlighting how steroids used for MG may not alleviate asthma symptoms. This underscores the critical need for careful evaluation of respiratory symptoms in MG patients, emphasizing the importance of integrating clinical history with targeted testing to avoid misdiagnosis and ensure effective treatment.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** Female in her 30s.
- **Chief Complaint:** Acute dry cough in bouts for one week.
- **History of Present Illness:** Ongoing muscle weakness for eight years, previously diagnosed with myasthenia gravis (MG), currently on steroids and rituximab for treatment.
- **Past Medical History:** Diagnosed with myasthenia gravis (MG), treated with intravenous rituximab cycles a month prior.
- **Personal History:** Obese (BMI 30.2 kg/m²), history of employment in a woodcarving factory, and reported lifelong allergic diathesis (episodic sneezing and rhinorrhea).
- **Family History:** None mentioned.
- **Physical Examination:** Vital signs normal, oxygen saturation at 97%. Respiratory system exam showed normal vesicular breath sounds, no adventitious sounds. No abnormalities on systemic exam.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Chest X-ray: Normal.
  - Complete blood count and eosinophil count: Normal.
  - Spirometry: Normal (FEV1/FVC 84.5%, FEV1% predicted 82 L, FVC% predicted 80 L).
  - High-resolution chest CT: Normal, no pleuro-parenchymal abnormality.
  - Transthoracic echocardiography: Normal, ejection fraction preserved.
  - Six-minute walk test: Could not be completed due to excessive coughing.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. Initial suspicion of an infectious etiology (bacterial, fungal, tubercular, or viral) due to immunosuppressed state from steroid and rituximab use. However, normal chest imaging, blood counts, and lung function tests effectively ruled out infection.
2. Consideration of asthma or its variants, particularly given the allergenic background and expositional history. Spirometry did not indicate obstruction or restriction, suggesting a variant rather than classical asthma.
3. Cough-Variant Asthma (CVA) was entertained due to the chronic dry cough as the primary symptom and history of allergic diathesis, episodic sneezing, and rhinorrhea since childhood.
4. The rapid and significant improvement in symptoms observed within 24 hours of initiating inhaled corticosteroids and long-acting beta-agonists supported CVA as the most likely diagnosis while excluding other potential conditions like chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, eosinophilic bronchitis, or gastroesophageal reflux disease.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: The final diagnosis is cough-variant asthma (CVA) in a patient with myasthenia gravis (MG) on immunomodulatory therapy. The diagnosis was supported by the patient’s significant response to targeted inhalation therapy of bronchodilators and steroids, in conjunction with a clinical history of allergic diathesis and exclusion of infectious or structural pulmonary disease.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by looking at this case. The patient is a woman in her 30s with a chief complaint of acute dry cough for a week. She has a history of myasthenia gravis (MG) for eight years, currently treated with steroids and rituximab. Her personal history includes obesity, working in a woodcarving factory, and allergies.

First, I need to consider the possible causes of a dry cough. Common causes include upper respiratory infections, allergies, asthma, GERD, or side effects from medications. But given her medical history, other factors might be at play.

Her MG is managed with rituximab, which is an immunosuppressant. Immunosuppression increases the risk of infections, even atypical ones. However, her chest X-ray and CT are normal, so pneumonia or other lung infections might not be the case. Blood counts are normal too, which might suggest it's not a bacterial infection. But maybe a viral infection? Though she doesn't have fever or other signs.

Another angle is drug-induced cough. Steroids can sometimes cause cough, but it's more common with ACE inhibitors. She isn't mentioned to be on those. Rituximab, though, has side effects including upper respiratory symptoms. Could this be a reaction to rituximab? Timing-wise, she had rituximab a month prior. Maybe a delayed reaction?

Her work in a woodcarving factory exposes her to dust and possible irritants. Could this be occupational asthma or hypersensitivity pneumonitis? However, spirometry is normal. Allergic diathesis might support asthma, but the normal FEV1/FVC ratio doesn't indicate obstruction. Maybe cough-variant asthma? But that typically shows some spirometric changes or response to bronchodilators.

GERD is another possibility, especially since obesity is a risk factor. Silent reflux can present with a chronic cough. However, this is acute (one week), so maybe less likely unless there's a recent exacerbation.

Her myasthenia gravis itself could affect respiratory muscles, but the cough is dry and she has normal oxygen saturation. Also, the six-minute walk test wasn't completed due to coughing, but the other tests are normal. Maybe it's not directly related to MG.

Considering all this, the most likely possibilities are: 1) Upper respiratory infection (viral), 2) Rituximab-induced cough, 3) Allergic or environmental irritation from her workplace, 4) GERD. Since imaging and spirometry are normal, infections and structural issues are less likely. The recent rituximab treatment could be a key factor here. Some reports link rituximab to upper respiratory symptoms and cough. Alternatively, her allergic history and occupational exposure could contribute to a cough, but the acute onset might align more with an infection or drug side effect. Given that she's immunosuppressed, even a mild viral infection could cause prolonged symptoms. But without other signs of infection, maybe drug-induced is more plausible. However, drug-induced coughs are not commonly listed for rituximab, though individual reactions can occur.

Another angle: Could this be a manifestation of myasthenic crisis? But she's on treatment, and the cough is acute without other MG symptoms mentioned. Probably not. Also, the cough is dry and not associated with weakness.

In conclusion, after ruling out common causes with normal tests, and considering her immunosuppressed state and medications, the cough might be due to a side effect of rituximab or an upper respiratory infection. However, given the normal imaging and labs, and the temporal association with rituximab (administered a month ago), perhaps a drug-induced interstitial lung disease, though CT was normal. Alternatively, considering her allergic history, maybe a post-viral cough or allergic rhinitis leading to postnasal drip. But no eosinophilia or imaging findings. Maybe the cough is a non-specific side effect of the medications, or a combination of factors like allergies and occupational exposure. 

Hmm, tough case. The key points are the acute dry cough in an immunocompromised patient with normal imaging and labs. Possible drug-related, or maybe a viral URI. Alternatively, consider cough-variant asthma or GERD, but tests are normal. The fact that the six-minute walk test was stopped due to coughing suggests it's severe. Maybe laryngeal involvement? Myasthenia can affect bulbar muscles, leading to aspiration or cough. But she's been stable on treatment. Could there be a new onset of bulbar weakness causing cough? But physical exam didn't mention any neurological findings. 

Wait, the physical exam states the respiratory system is normal, no adventitious sounds. So maybe not aspiration. 

Alternatively, consider that rituximab can cause hypersensitivity reactions, including cough. Even though it's a month later, maybe a delayed reaction. Or perhaps a late-onset side effect like interstitial lung disease, but CT is normal. 

Another thought: Obesity can contribute to cough via GERD or obstructive sleep apnea, but she's had this cough for a week, so acute. 

I think I need to focus on the most probable diagnosis given the information. The patient is on rituximab, which is a B-cell depleting therapy. Immunosuppression could make her prone to infections, but all tests are negative. Alternatively, the cough could be a non-specific side effect. But in the absence of other findings, maybe the diagnosis is upper airway cough syndrome (postnasal drip) related to her allergic diathesis. Or a viral URI. However, without rhinorrhea or other symptoms, maybe not. 

Wait, her chief complaint is dry cough. Allergic postnasal drip might cause a tickle and cough, but she has episodic sneezing and rhinorrhea. Maybe she's having an allergic flare leading to postnasal drip and cough. However, the cough is acute for a week. Maybe a new allergen exposure at work? Wood dust could be an irritant. Even with normal imaging, irritant-induced cough is possible. 

So possible causes: allergic/postnasal drip, irritant-induced cough from occupational exposure, drug-induced, or viral URI. Given the normal tests, the most likely is upper airway cough syndrome (UACS) from allergies or irritants. Alternatively, considering her immunosuppression, maybe a mild viral infection not showing on imaging. But without other symptoms, it's hard to say. 

In the end, the diagnosis might be upper airway cough syndrome secondary to allergic diathesis and occupational exposure. Alternatively, acute viral upper respiratory infection. But given the normal exam and tests, UACS is plausible. However, the answer needs to be precise. Another possibility is that the cough is a side effect of rituximab, though that's less common. 

Alternatively, could it be a manifestation of interstitial lung disease from rituximab? But CT is normal. Hmm. 

Alternatively, maybe the steroids are causing some issues. Steroids can cause GERD, which can lead to cough. But she's on steroids long-term, so an acute change might not be related. 

I think I need to go with the most likely diagnosis based on the information. The patient's history of allergies and occupational exposure points towards an irritant or allergic cause. Given the normal imaging and spirometry, upper airway cough syndrome (postnasal drip) due to allergic rhinitis exacerbated by occupational exposure seems possible. However, the acute onset might suggest a viral infection. But without fever or other signs, maybe not. 

Alternatively, considering her immunosuppression, could she have a atypical infection like pertussis? But the cough is a week old, and pertussis usually has a longer course. 

In the absence of other findings, maybe the diagnosis is upper airway cough syndrome secondary to allergic rhinitis and occupational irritant exposure. But the answer is supposed to be the final diagnosis. Alternatively, if everything is normal, maybe the cough is related to her myasthenia gravis. But how? Myasthenia typically doesn't cause cough unless there's bulbar involvement leading to aspiration. But the exam didn't show any signs. 

Alternatively, maybe the cough is psychogenic, but that's a diagnosis of exclusion. 

Wait, the six-minute walk test couldn't be completed due to coughing. That suggests the cough is triggered by exertion. Could that be a clue? Exercise-induced bronchoconstriction (asthma) can present with cough. But spirometry was normal. However, spirometry might not capture exercise-induced symptoms. Maybe she has exercise-induced asthma, which wasn't detected in the spirometry. So, the diagnosis could be cough-variant asthma or exercise-induced bronchoconstriction. But the spirometry is normal. However, methacholine challenge test wasn't done. 

Alternatively, since she couldn't complete the walk test due to coughing, maybe that's a sign of hyperreactive airways. So, possible asthma. But spirometry is normal. Maybe she needs a bronchodilator challenge or FeNO test. But those weren't done. 

Given the information available, maybe the most accurate diagnosis is upper airway cough syndrome (allergic rhinitis) with possible contribution from occupational irritants. Alternatively, considering the acute onset and lack of findings, a viral upper respiratory infection. However, given that she's on immunosuppressants, even a mild infection could present with persistent cough. 

But the answer requires a single diagnosis. The key factors are her immunosuppression, normal imaging, acute cough. Considering the medications, rituximab's side effects include upper respiratory symptoms, including cough. So, possible drug-induced cough. But I need to check if that's a known side effect. Rituximab can cause infusion-related reactions, but these usually occur during or shortly after infusion. However, delayed reactions can happen. There are case reports of interstitial lung disease with rituximab, but CT here is normal. 

Alternatively, since she's on steroids, maybe the dose is being tapered, leading to a rebound inflammation. But that's speculative. 

Alternatively, given her occupation, maybe occupational asthma, but spirometry is normal. 

In the end, with normal tests and her history, the most likely diagnosis is upper airway cough syndrome due to allergic rhinitis and/or occupational irritant exposure. But if I have to choose based on the given information, maybe that's the answer. Alternatively, the cough could be a side effect of rituximab. However, without more evidence, it's hard. 

Wait, but the case mentions that the CT and X-ray are normal. So, no interstitial lung disease. Blood tests are normal. So, probably not an infection. Then, considering the allergies and occupation, the diagnosis might be upper airway cough syndrome (UACS). The answer would be UACS. Alternatively, considering the acute nature and the fact that she's immunosuppressed, maybe viral. But since labs and imaging are normal, UACS is more likely. 

But another angle: Myasthenia gravis can lead to respiratory muscle weakness, but the cough is dry and there's no evidence of respiratory distress. So probably not. 

Final thought: Given the history of allergies (allergic diathesis) and occupational exposure to wood dust, the most plausible diagnosis is upper airway cough syndrome (postnasal drip) secondary to allergic rhinitis exacerbated by occupational irritants. Alternatively, the dry cough could be due to GERD, but there's no mention of heartburn. 

But the answer needs to be precise. Let's check standard differentials for acute dry cough in immunocompromised. 

Common causes: viral URI, postnasal drip, asthma, GERD, ACE inhibitors (she's not on them), interstitial lung disease (but CT normal). 

Since all tests are normal, likely UACS or viral URI. But since it's a week and no other symptoms, maybe UACS. 

So, the answer would be upper airway cough syndrome (UACS) due to allergic rhinitis and/or occupational irritant exposure. Alternatively, acute viral upper respiratory infection. But the case says "acute dry cough in bouts for one week" with no other symptoms. So, given her allergic history and occupation, UACS seems more likely.
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient’s acute dry cough occurs in the context of immunosuppression (rituximab and steroids) and occupational exposure to wood dust. Common infectious etiologies (e.g., pneumonia) are less likely given normal chest imaging, labs, and preserved oxygen saturation.  
<step 2> Drug-induced cough is considered, but rituximab’s association with cough is uncommon and typically linked to acute infusion reactions rather than delayed onset. Steroids may contribute to GERD, but acute cough without typical reflux symptoms reduces this likelihood.  
<step 3> Allergic diathesis and occupational irritant exposure (wood dust) suggest upper airway cough syndrome (UACS) due to postnasal drip from allergic rhinitis or environmental triggers. Normal spirometry and imaging exclude asthma or parenchymal lung disease.  
<step 4> Cough-variant asthma or exercise-induced bronchoconstriction is possible, but normal spirometry and lack of wheezing make this less likely. The inability to complete the six-minute walk test could reflect cough exacerbation by exertion, aligning with UACS or airway hyperreactivity.  
<step 5> Myasthenia gravis-related complications (e.g., bulbar weakness or aspiration) are unlikely given normal physical exam and lack of neurological findings.  
<step 6> After excluding infections, structural abnormalities, and medication effects, the most probable diagnosis is upper airway cough syndrome secondary to allergic rhinitis and occupational irritant exposure.  

### Answer:
Upper airway cough syndrome (UACS) secondary to allergic rhinitis and occupational irritant exposure.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient’s acute dry cough occurs in the context of immunosuppression (rituximab and steroids) and occupational exposure to wood dust. Common infectious etiologies (e.g., pneumonia) are less likely given normal chest imaging, labs, and preserved oxygen saturation.

Drug-induced cough is considered, but rituximab’s association with cough is uncommon and typically linked to acute infusion reactions rather than delayed onset. Steroids may contribute to GERD, but acute cough without typical reflux symptoms reduces this likelihood.

Allergic diathesis and occupational irritant exposure (wood dust) suggest upper airway cough syndrome (UACS) due to postnasal drip from allergic rhinitis or environmental triggers. Normal spirometry and imaging exclude asthma or parenchymal lung disease.

Cough-variant asthma or exercise-induced bronchoconstriction is possible, but normal spirometry and lack of wheezing make this less likely. The inability to complete the six-minute walk test could reflect cough exacerbation by exertion, aligning with UACS or airway hyperreactivity.
Myasthenia gravis-related complications (e.g., bulbar weakness or aspiration) are unlikely given normal physical exam and lack of neurological findings.

After excluding infections, structural abnormalities, and medication effects, the most probable diagnosis is upper airway cough syndrome secondary to allergic rhinitis and occupational irritant exposure.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Initial suspicion of an infectious etiology (bacterial, fungal, tubercular, or viral) due to immunosuppressed state from steroid and rituximab use, but ruled out by normal chest imaging, blood counts, and lung function tests.
Consideration of asthma or its variants given allergenic background and expositional history; spirometry did not indicate obstruction or restriction.
Entertainment of Cough-Variant Asthma (CVA) due to chronic dry cough as primary symptom, history of allergic diathesis, episodic sneezing, and rhinorrhea since childhood.
Rapid and significant improvement in symptoms within 24 hours of inhaled corticosteroids and long-acting beta-agonists supports CVA as the most likely diagnosis.
Exclusion of other potential conditions like chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, eosinophilic bronchitis, or gastroesophageal reflux disease.
Final diagnosis: Cough-variant asthma (CVA) in a patient with myasthenia gravis (MG) on immunomodulatory therapy.
Diagnosis supported by significant response to targeted inhalation therapy of bronchodilators and steroids, along with clinical history and exclusion of infectious or structural pulmonary disease.
